text: This is an IMAS educational video on robotic myomectomy for a large uterine fibroid. The patient was a thirty-year-old female who presented to us with complaints of heaviness in hypochondrium and primary infertility. On examination, a lump was palpable reaching up to the infraumblycal region. On MRI there was a large uterine fundal fibroid approximately thirteen into ten centimeters in size.

The surgical team prepares for a robotic myomectomy by draping the patient cart and planning port placements. The camera port is positioned above the umbilicus in the midline, while robotic ports are placed in the right and left midclavicular lines, forming a triangular configuration with the camera port. The patient cart is docked from the left thigh for side docking, allowing space for an assistant between the legs. Two assistant ports are positioned in the right and left hypochondrium for myoma screw and suction irrigation. The camera arm and instrument arms are docked following diagnostic laparoscopy.

During diagnostic laparoscopy, a large fundal fibroid is visible, with normal-appearing fallopian tubes and ovaries. The surgeon inserts a myoma screw into the fibroid for manipulation, applying traction and counter traction. Vasopressin is injected around the fibroid to reduce vascularity, causing visible blanching. A permanent cautery hook and fenestrated bipolar forceps are used to identify and develop a surgical plane around the fibroid, minimizing bleeding and avoiding injury to the adnexa. The fibroid protrudes from its coverings as the surgeon dissects using robotic instruments, relying on visual feedback to assess force application.

The surgeon reapplies the myoma screw multiple times to provide traction and counter traction, uncovering the myoma as they proceed towards the uterine fundus. Once the myoma is fully separated, it is divided from its stalk using a harmonic scalpel after the robot is undocked. Hemostasis is confirmed, and the myoma is extracted through morselation, taking care to avoid injury to visceral organs or bowel. After extraction, the peritoneal covering is closed, hemostasis is confirmed, and the ports are removed under vision.
